industries.name: Paleoceanography & Nuclear Research
uses: Protactinium-231 (²³¹Pa, t½ = 32,760 years) is used in geochronology as part of the ²³¹Pa/²³⁰Th dating method for marine sediments and coral — the Pa/Th ratio tracks ocean circulation changes over the past 300,000 years, providing key evidence for past changes in the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC)

²³¹Pa/²³⁰Th ratios in deep-sea sediment cores are among the most powerful tracers of past ocean circulation — the method revealed that AMOC slowed dramatically during the Younger Dryas cold period (~12,900 years ago) and other glacial stadials

Protactinium-233 (²³³Pa, t½ = 27 days) is a critical intermediate in the thorium fuel cycle: ²³²Th + n → ²³³Th → ²³³Pa → ²³³U (fissile). Pa-233 must be continuously removed from molten salt thorium reactors to prevent neutron losses
World supply of purified ²³¹Pa is only ~125 grams, held primarily at Chalk River Laboratories (Canada) — one of the rarest separated elements on Earth
Protactinium was first isolated in macroscopic quantities in 1934 by Aristid von Grosse — separating ~0.1mg from several tons of uranium ore residue in a months-long chemical effort

companies.name: Canadian Nuclear Laboratories (Chalk River)
companies.use: Holds the world's largest purified protactinium-231 stockpile (~125g), originally separated from uranium processing residues. Supplies Pa-231 to oceanographic and paleoclimate research institutions for sediment core dating and ocean circulation studies.

companies.name: Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution
companies.use: World leader in ²³¹Pa/²³⁰Th paleoceanographic research. Measures Pa/Th ratios in Atlantic sediment cores to reconstruct past AMOC strength over glacial-interglacial cycles — one of the primary scientific consumers of protactinium-231 analytical standards and reference materials.

companies.name: ETH Zürich (Geological Institute)
companies.use: Major center for uranium-series and Pa/Th geochronology applied to coral dating, cave deposits (speleothems), and marine sediments. Uses Pa-231 reference standards for mass spectrometric measurements of past ocean circulation changes and sea level history.
